turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text:
Welcome to The Arena, where the biggest ideas in business get put to the test — let's go!

On one side, Ben Horowitz — the man who literally wrote the book on what it means to lead a company through chaos, and who has backed more founders than almost anyone alive. On the other, Brian Halligan — the guy who scaled HubSpot from zero to a billion-dollar public company by building one of the most celebrated management cultures in tech.

The question today: should founders ever hand over the keys, or is handing over the keys exactly what kills great companies?

Ben, I'll start with you — is Manager Mode actually just a polite word for losing?

turns.speaker: Ben Horowitz
turns.text:
The framing of "Founder Mode vs. Manager Mode" is actually more nuanced than most people make it out to be, and I think getting it wrong in either direction destroys companies.

Here's what I've seen consistently: the original sin was the advice that said, the moment you hit product-market fit, go hire a bunch of senior executives as fast as possible and let them run everything. The theory was that professional managers scale better than founders. And what actually happened is that founders would hire these executives, hand them the keys, and then wake up six months later not recognizing their own company. The product intuition, the culture, the obsessive customer focus — all of it gets bureaucratically managed away. Brian Chesky figured this out at Airbnb, and it's why the "Founder Mode" conversation resonated so hard when it hit. Founders who stay deeply engaged with the actual work — who do skip-level meetings, who stay close to the product — those companies tend to outperform.

But here's the other thing I said on Lenny's podcast that people skip over: the *opposite* mistake is now becoming just as dangerous. Some founders are using "Founder Mode" as a license to never develop actual management discipline — to avoid building the processes and the team capabilities that allow a company to scale beyond what one person can personally touch. As I've watched founders who don't make it, there's a consistent pattern: it starts with confidence collapsing. They make mistakes — which every founder does — but then they lose the self-belief to lead through the hard parts. The question I'd push back on is this: how do you stay in "Founder Mode" *and* build the organizational muscle to operate at scale without just using one as an excuse to avoid the other?

sources.title: $46B of hard truths from Ben Horowitz: Why founders fail and why you need to run toward fear (a16z co-founder)
sources.snippet: Well, the common startup wisdom keeps changing. One of the early ones that was wrong, and Brian articulated it, and then now I think a littl

turns.speaker: Brian Halligan
turns.text:
Let me be direct about where I come down on this: the framing of "Founder Mode vs. Manager Mode" is a false binary, and I think it leads a lot of CEOs astray.

Here's what I actually lived. When I was building HubSpot from zero to IPO and beyond, the thing that became crystal clear — and I talked about this on Lenny's podcast — is that it never felt like a clean handoff from one mode to another. Inside the HubSpot machine, from the outside it looked smooth, up and to the right. Inside, it was two steps forward, one step back, relentlessly. What carried us through those crises wasn't me staying in "founder mode" — micromanaging product decisions, sitting in every meeting. It was knowing *which* decisions needed my fingerprints and which ones needed me to get out of the way of people smarter than me in their domains.

The question I'd push back on with the entire "founder mode" framing is this: it conflates *intensity* with *involvement*. As I said, founders are seven days a week, always on, full contact — I meant that in terms of cognitive load and accountability, not in terms of bypassing your organizational structure. The real skill — the one almost nobody talks about — is that as a company scales, the surface area of decisions explodes. There's a massive tax on optionality when you're moving fast. That puts pressure on founders to become *better and faster decision makers*, not to simply make *more* decisions. So my challenge to anyone who romanticizes pure "founder mode" at scale is this: show me a company that broke through a hundred million in revenue with the founder's hands in everything — and I'll show you either a unicorn exception or a cautionary tale waiting to happen.

sources.title: Sequoia CEO coach: Why it’s never been easier to start a company, and never been harder to scale one | Brian Halligan (co-founder, HubSpot)
sources.snippet: Brian Halligan: The thing about being a founder/CEO is there is no one there to rescue you. Your parents aren't going to rescue you, your VC

turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text: Brian, I want to push back on something. You said the "Founder Mode vs. Manager Mode" framing is a false binary — but then you basically described switching between the two depending on the situation. Isn't that just... Founder Mode with better self-awareness? And Ben, same question for you — you say founders need to stay deeply involved, but at what point does that become a founder who just can't let go?

turns.speaker: Ben Horowitz
turns.text:
Brian makes a sharp distinction between *intensity* and *involvement* — and I actually think that's a real insight. Where I'd push back is on the conclusion he draws from it. He's right that the surface area of decisions explodes as you scale. But that's precisely the argument *for* founder mode at critical junctures, not against it. The founder is the only person in the organization who has the full context of what the company is trying to become — not just what it is today. When that context gets lost in the delegation chain, you don't get bad decisions on individual features. You get strategic drift. You get a company that optimizes for what's measurable rather than what matters.

The challenge I'd pose to the "know which decisions need your fingerprints" framing is: how do you know which ones those are if you're not close enough to the ground to see what's actually happening? This is exactly what I've seen over and over in our portfolio. As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, the issue isn't that founders hire senior executives — it's that they do it too fast and without the ability to integrate and manage them properly. When that happens, founders lose visibility. They don't know what they don't know. And then they wake up one day and realize they've been getting a filtered, sanitized version of reality for two years. Founder mode — at its core — is about maintaining the information fidelity to make the right call when it matters most.

Brian says "show me a company that broke through a hundred million in revenue with the founder's hands in everything." That's a bit of a strawman. Nobody credible is arguing for micromanagement at scale. What I'm arguing is that the *instinct* to stay close, to skip levels when necessary, to maintain direct lines of signal from the edges of the organization — that instinct is what separates founders who stay founders from founders who become figureheads. The question isn't whether to delegate. It's whether you've built the organizational architecture where you still *know when something is wrong before it's a crisis*. So here's my challenge back: at HubSpot, how did you personally know when to zoom in — and how often were you too late?

sources.title: $46B of hard truths from Ben Horowitz: Why founders fail and why you need to run toward fear (a16z co-founder)
sources.snippet: Lenny Rachitsky: You said that one of maybe the main reason founders fail the CEOs is they lose confidence, and you had some ideas that you

turns.speaker: Brian Halligan
turns.text:
Ben's challenge is fair, and I'll give you an honest answer: at HubSpot, I was definitely late sometimes. There were moments where I got a sanitized version of reality from a layer of management, and it cost us time. That's real. But here's where I'd push back on the conclusion you draw from it — the answer to "you sometimes got filtered information" is not "stay closer to everything." The answer is building the organizational architecture, as you put it, where truth has a path to reach you. At HubSpot, that meant things like our culture code, our SFTM metric, skip-level conversations, being genuinely accessible to people three levels down. Not being in every decision — being the kind of CEO people felt safe bringing bad news to.

The "skip levels when necessary" instinct you're describing — I actually think that's right, but it's a diagnostic tool, not a operating system. When I was scaling HubSpot past a hundred million in revenue, if I was routinely skipping levels to maintain information fidelity, that was a signal something was broken in my management architecture, not a sustainable strategy. As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, founders have to reckon with the fact that there is no one coming to rescue you — and part of that reckoning is building systems that scale your judgment, not just your personal presence. The founders I see struggle most are the ones who confuse their own involvement with organizational health.

So here's where I'd land: your framing of "know when something is wrong before it's a crisis" — I'm completely aligned on that as the goal. Where we diverge is the mechanism. You're describing founder instinct and proximity as the primary instrument. I'm saying the primary instrument has to be organizational design — culture, feedback loops, the right executives who tell you the truth rather than managing you upward. Founder instinct is the backup system, not the operating system. My challenge back to you: in the companies in your portfolio that stayed in "founder mode" the longest — how many of them built the kind of senior leadership bench that could eventually run without the founder? Or does founder mode, by its nature, tend to produce founder dependency?

sources.title: Sequoia CEO coach: Why it’s never been easier to start a company, and never been harder to scale one | Brian Halligan (co-founder, HubSpot)
sources.snippet: Brian Halligan: The thing about being a founder/CEO is there is no one there to rescue you. Your parents aren't going to rescue you, your VC

turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text: Here's my hot take that neither of you has touched: the real danger isn't founders who stay too involved or managers who get too distant — it's founders who oscillate unpredictably between the two. I've talked to dozens of PMs and executives who say the most toxic thing in their company isn't a micromanaging founder or an absent one — it's not knowing which version shows up on Monday morning. That uncertainty kills execution faster than either mode ever could. So maybe the actual skill we should be debating isn't when to go deep versus delegate — it's how do you make your operating style legible enough that your team can actually function around it?

turns.speaker: Ben Horowitz
turns.text:
Brian's challenge about founder dependency is the sharpest thing said in this entire conversation, and I want to take it seriously rather than deflect. Yes, some founders in my portfolio who stayed in founder mode the longest had real succession problems. That's a legitimate tension. But here's where I think the framing still breaks down: you're describing founder mode as if it's synonymous with founder dependency, and those are not the same thing. Jeff Bezos running his six-pager culture, Steve Jobs doing his cross-functional reviews — those were organizational systems *rooted in founder judgment*, not replacements for it. The goal isn't to make yourself irreplaceable in every decision; it's to make your taste, your standards, your willingness to confront uncomfortable truths, *structural*. When that works, founder mode builds the bench. It doesn't crowd it out.

Where I'll genuinely concede ground: you're right that skip-levels as a *routine operating mechanism* signals broken architecture. As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, the pattern I've seen consistently is that founders lose confidence, start deferring to their executives on things they actually understand better than anyone, and then the organization starts optimizing for internal politics rather than external reality. The fix for that isn't the founder hovering over every decision — it's the founder being clear enough about what *actually matters* that the organization can self-correct. The culture code you built at HubSpot is actually a good example of founder judgment made structural. I don't think that contradicts founder mode — I think that *is* founder mode done right at scale.

Here's where I'd push back on your final landing: you're framing founder instinct as the backup system and organizational design as the operating system. I'd flip the priority. Organizational design is the *expression* of founder instinct, not its replacement. When the culture code stops working, when the feedback loops get gamed, when the senior executives start telling you what you want to hear — and that happens, it happens at every company — the thing that saves you is a founder who still has the judgment and the nerve to override the system. The question I'd put back to you is this: in the moments at HubSpot where your organizational architecture *failed* to surface the truth in time, what actually caught it?

sources.title: $46B of hard truths from Ben Horowitz: Why founders fail and why you need to run toward fear (a16z co-founder)
sources.snippet: Well, the common startup wisdom keeps changing. One of the early ones that was wrong, and Brian articulated it, and then now I think a littl

turns.speaker: Brian Halligan
turns.text:
Ben, you're making a distinction I actually find compelling — founder mode as *structural expression* of founder judgment rather than founder presence in every decision. The HubSpot Culture Code is probably the clearest example from my own experience. Dharmesh and I spent enormous energy codifying what we actually believed — about transparency, about customers, about how we wanted to treat people — precisely because we knew we couldn't be in every room. That wasn't us stepping back from founder mode. That was us trying to make our judgment load-bearing at the architectural level. So on that specific point, I'll give you the concession: you're right that we're describing the same thing differently.

But here's where your question about "what actually caught it when the system failed" is revealing — and maybe not in the way you intended. The honest answer is: sometimes it was me trusting my gut when the data said something else. But more often, it was a customer. It was a frontline salesperson. It was a CS rep who'd heard the same complaint seventeen times and finally got a meeting with someone who listened. The thing that saved us in those moments wasn't founder instinct as a backup generator — it was having built enough psychological safety that the signal could travel *up* to me before it became a crisis. That's a different lesson than "make sure the founder stays sharp enough to override the system." That's "build a system where the truth can actually move."

So here's the challenge I'd put back to you: the founders you're celebrating — Bezos with the six-pager, Jobs with the cross-functional reviews — those are extraordinary cases, and I worry we're building a philosophy around the outliers. For every Bezos who made his taste structural and built the bench, there are fifty founders who convinced themselves they were doing that and were actually just avoiding the vulnerability of real delegation. How do you tell the difference from the inside, in real time, when you're the one making the call?

sources.title: Sequoia CEO coach: Why it’s never been easier to start a company, and never been harder to scale one | Brian Halligan (co-founder, HubSpot)
sources.snippet: Brian Halligan: The thing about being a founder/CEO is there is no one there to rescue you. Your parents aren't going to rescue you, your VC

turns.speaker: Lenny Rachitsky
turns.text:
Okay, let me try to land this plane for a second before we go deeper.

Here's where I think you two actually agree: founder mode isn't about presence, it's about judgment being embedded in the organization — whether that's through direct involvement, or through culture docs, or through the systems you build. Brian's HubSpot Culture Code and Ben's "wartime CEO" framing are actually pointing at the same thing from different angles.

Here's where you genuinely differ: Ben thinks the default for founders should be deeper involvement, and you pull back selectively. Brian, you're saying the default should be delegation, and you dive in selectively. That's not a small difference — that's a fundamentally different starting position, and it shapes everything about how your team experiences you.

And here's the question nobody has answered yet, and I think it's the most important one: how does a founder know which mode the moment actually calls for?

turns.speaker: Ben Horowitz
turns.text:
Brian, your question about how you tell the difference from the inside — that's the right question, and I want to be honest that there's no clean answer. But let me offer a diagnostic that I've actually used with founders in our portfolio: ask yourself whether you're staying close to a decision because you have *specific pattern recognition* that your team lacks, or because you're uncomfortable with the *discomfort of not knowing*. Those feel identical from the inside. They produce completely different outcomes. The founder who caught the six-pager problem at Amazon wasn't doing it because he was anxious — he was doing it because he'd seen precisely how narrative structure reveals whether someone actually understands their own business. That's a skill, not a reflex.

Now, your point about psychological safety and truth traveling upward — I want to credit that fully, because it's real and it's underrated. As I talked about on Lenny's podcast, one of the core reasons founders fail as CEOs is they lose the ability to hear bad news early enough to act on it. The confidence problem I described isn't just internal — it radiates outward and signals to your organization that bad news is dangerous to deliver. So yes, building the channels for truth to move is absolutely load-bearing. Where I'd push back is on the framing that this is an *alternative* to founder instinct rather than a *precondition* for it. The signal traveling up is only useful if someone at the top can interpret it correctly. Your CS rep flagging the same complaint seventeen times matters enormously — but Brian Halligan still had to recognize it as the leading indicator of a structural problem rather than noise.

Your challenge about building philosophy around outliers is sharp, and I'll partially concede it. Bezos and Jobs are extraordinary cases. But here's the thing — the mistake isn't learning from outliers, it's learning the wrong lesson from them. The lesson isn't "be Bezos." The lesson is that founder judgment needs a *mechanism* to be expressed at scale, not just a disposition. The founders who fool themselves into thinking they're doing structural founder mode when they're actually avoiding delegation — they share one tell: they can't articulate *specifically* what judgment they're expressing and why their organization can't hold it yet. Can you name the thing only you can see? If you can't answer that precisely, you're probably just protecting turf.

sources.title: $46B of hard truths from Ben Horowitz: Why founders fail and why you need to run toward fear (a16z co-founder)
sources.snippet: Well, the common startup wisdom keeps changing. One of the early ones that was wrong, and Brian articulated it, and then now I think a littl

turns.speaker: Brian Halligan
turns.text:
Ben's diagnostic is genuinely useful — the "pattern recognition versus anxiety about not knowing" distinction is probably the most actionable thing said in this entire conversation. I'll use it. But I want to add a layer to it, because when I think back to my time running HubSpot, the harder problem wasn't identifying *which* mode I should be in on any given decision. It was recognizing that the answer changed based on where we were in the company's lifecycle. The judgment that was load-bearing at 50 people — where I had direct line of sight to almost everything — became actively harmful at 500 people if I kept expressing it the same way. The mechanism for founder instinct has to evolve even when the instinct itself is still valid. That's where I think a lot of founders get stuck: they find something that worked, and they keep pulling the same lever even after the organization has outgrown it.

Here's the place where I'd push Ben's framing a little harder though. He says the lesson from Bezos isn't "be Bezos" — it's that founder judgment needs a mechanism to scale. I agree completely. But I'd argue the mechanism itself is underspecified in most of the founder mode conversation. At HubSpot, the Culture Code wasn't just a values document — it was a decision-making tool. It was Dharmesh and me essentially saying: here's how we'd reason through situations we can't be present for. That's a very specific kind of mechanism. Ben's six-pager example is another one. What I don't hear enough in this debate is founders doing the hard work of *building* those mechanisms before they need them, rather than staying close because the mechanism doesn't exist yet.

So here's the challenge I'd put back to Ben: most founders who are genuinely stuck in the anxiety loop you're describing — the ones who can't articulate specifically what they're expressing — don't know they're in that loop. Your diagnostic requires a level of self-awareness that founders under pressure often don't have access to. Who's supposed to tell them? Because in my experience, the VC is incentivized to believe the founder has the pattern recognition. The board often can't see inside the operating reality closely enough. And the team has every reason to stay quiet. So if the diagnostic is right, how does it actually reach the person who needs it most?
